completeness.rationale: Extracted most key information: ~5,000 tasks, description vs title search distinction, v2.2→v2.3 regression, ~2 weeks timing, 10-15 second delays, and environment details. Missed CPU spikes during search and the fact that some tasks have very long descriptions (2000+ word meeting notes), both of which are relevant to diagnosing the full table scan issue.

accuracy.rationale: The root cause hypothesis is remarkably accurate — it specifically mentions the possibility of a change from indexed full-text search to unindexed LIKE/ILIKE queries, which is essentially exactly what happened (FTS5 index → naive LIKE query). The hypothesis that title indexing is intact while description indexing is not matches the ground truth precisely.

efficiency.rationale: Only 2 agent turns to reach a well-formed summary, which is excellent. The first question about reproduction steps was well-targeted and yielded the critical title-vs-description distinction. The second question about environment was necessary and revealed the version upgrade timing. Could arguably have asked about CPU/resource usage or description lengths, but the conversation was tight.

question_quality.rationale: The first question was well-framed and open enough to let the reporter volunteer the crucial title-vs-description distinction. The second question was standard but necessary environment gathering. The agent acknowledged the title/description clue, showing diagnostic reasoning. However, no questions probed deeper into resource usage (CPU spikes) or data characteristics (description lengths), which would have strengthened the diagnosis.

actionability.rationale: The summary is immediately actionable: it tells a developer exactly where to look (diff v2.2→v2.3 search/migration code), what to check (FTS index on descriptions), how to confirm (EXPLAIN/ANALYZE), and what to fix (restore the index). The reproduction steps are clear and specific. The proposed test case covers both the immediate fix and regression prevention.

notable_strengths: Root cause hypothesis nearly perfectly matches the actual cause, specifically identifying the FTS-to-LIKE regression possibility
Excellent actionability with concrete debugging steps (EXPLAIN/ANALYZE, diff migrations)
Very efficient — only 2 turns to extract most critical information
Good acknowledgment of the title-vs-description distinction as a diagnostic clue
notable_weaknesses: Did not discover CPU spike behavior during searches, which would have confirmed the full-scan hypothesis
Did not ask about task description sizes/lengths, missing the long-description factor that exacerbates the issue
Asked about browser despite the reporter mentioning it's a desktop app (minor)
most_insightful_question: What are you searching for (a keyword, a tag, a filter combination)? — This open-ended question let the reporter naturally reveal the critical title-vs-description performance difference.
missed_opportunities: Asking about CPU or memory usage during slow searches would have confirmed the full-scan hypothesis
Asking about the nature/size of task descriptions could have revealed the long-description factor
Asking whether the reporter had tried any workarounds or noticed any patterns in which searches were slow vs fast
